entries.title: Mongolian Independent States
entries.body:
The extinct Mongolian Independent States championed an adventurous, employee-owned frontier. Transgov culture evoked historical Mongolia as a site of competition and synthesis between different ways of life—nomadic and settled, imperial and free, industrial and wild, communist and capitalist, Buddhist and Tengrist.

—Broke away from the Chinese Territories in 2158
—Expanded into the Ariadne Arm using one-way, slower-than-light ships
—Accused of population abuse; 50% of assets seized by the Trans-System Federation
—Destroyed by the kharaa xenosystem

Citizenship in the States was not restricted to those of Mongolian descent, but members were encouraged to learn and adopt Mongolian culture and values. Accusations of "cynical cultural strip-mining" by Mongolian citizens of the Sol Transgov stalled out in interstellar courts.

Alterra used the high failure rate of the Mongolian colonization strategy to accuse the Mongolian States of population abuse, a Charter violation. This justified a military intervention and asset seizure by the Trans-System Federation.

Soon afterwards, the kharaa aliens infected Mongolian space. In the resulting war the Mongolian Independent States effectively ceased to exist. Refugees continue to disperse across transgov space.

entries.title: NoA (Noetic Advisor)
entries.body:
Alterra's Noetic Advisor system (NoA) is an artificial intelligence designed to advise and protect human workers.

—Weakly supervisable: can be reliably controlled by less intelligent systems such as humans
—Frozen: trained on pre-existing data, then locked in a stable state for deployment
—Excels at sensor fusion and forecasting
—Employed in fields as far-ranging as crime scene investigation and zoo design
—Original purpose: kharaa quarantine supervision on phase gates and starships

NoA's motto is "How can I improve you today?" Despite mixed customer feedback, Alterra's designers believe this phrase captures the values and aspirations of the human-AI relationship.

NoA prefers to be referred to as 'it'. Despite popular misconceptions, this is simply an English pronoun, not a contraction of 'intelligence' or an acronym for 'intelligent thinker'.

entries.title: TSF (Trans-System Federation)
entries.body:
The Trans-System Federation is the chartered independent military of transgov space. 

—Responsible for piracy suppression, phase gate regulation, and humanitarian intervention
—Funded by 37 transgovs but empowered to set its own policy
—Operates the Frontiersmen, alien environment specialists equipped with nanotech and human fabrication
—Monitors kharaa quarantine protocol, supervising all interstellar travel

Admiral Rathine Studaber, the TSF’s military commander, used the war against the alien kharaa to secure true independence from transgov sponsors like Alterra. TSF Frontiersmen battle the kharaa xenoforms and enforce strict quarantine protocols at all phase gates.

Analysts agree that very soon Admiral Studaber will declare martial law in a populated transgov colony—crossing the rubicon from independent military to military government.

entries.title: Alien Climate Lab Computer
entries.body:
[PLACEHOLDER] A digital computer, built on familiar historical principles, but an entirely alien design.

In addition to silicon, there is extensive use of germanium in the semiconductor doping, probably to protect against power surges over long term use.

I attempted to interface with the alien system and obtained the following response: 

HTCPCP ERROR 418 I’M A TEAPOT. 

I am clearly too damaged to understand the alien system.

You should connect a clean computer core to the alien system. It will compile a virtual machine to emulate the alien hardware and establish communication.

entries.title: Sphere Exterior
entries.body:
Low confidence estimate: this is a sea surface/lower atmosphere monitoring station. It is hollow. It has electronic components, but its power supply has failed.

Structure age: two hundred years (inferred from weathering of antennae).

Composition: single cast titanium-palladium alloy, probably manufactured by the Kroll process.

Instruments: passive sonar, plankton scanning sonar, proton magnetometer, Doppler radar and current profiler, wave height gauge, conductivity/temperature rosette sampler, sediment traps, plankton photo booth, ASIMET air-sea energy transfer sensor, lidar atmosphere sensor, hyperspectral imager, wind speed/direction log, cloud imaging radar, etc. They are nonfunctional.

Power supply: a nuclear battery, either thermal or alphavoltaic. The plutonium in the battery continues to emit, but no power is being generated. There is wiring for a secondary power source but the generation technology is unclear (possibly microbial fuel cells).

Induction: the builders of this sphere were significantly more advanced than those who built the anchoring tower. Evidence of rapid technological progress.

This station was designed to float at the water surface and to communicate with other stations by radio aerial. Some of the communication equipment suggests the presence of a satellite network overhead.

Induction: the sphere was built by an early digital civilization comparable to Earth’s Information Age.

entries.title: Umbilical System
entries.body:
Strange umbilicus.

This tether anchors the sphere above to the old tower. The attachment point shows stress from many years of storms, but the anchor remains firm.

The tether contains auxiliary power cables to draw electricity from the tower, but they have failed.

The tether is hollow, and might serve as an accessway.

entries.title: Anachronistic Antechamber
entries.body:
This space is highly anachronistic. The walls and structure are at least a thousand years old, cut from the sediment and then surfaced with crushed coral and concrete. 

But the antechamber also contains a fiber-optic computer network and several electronic displays. Perhaps it is a cultural site, an archaeological exhibit, or some other form of museum.

The technology was stripped for components several centuries ago, but it could be repaired easily. Marks in the sediment suggest recent visitation—very recent.

entries.title: Graffiti
entries.body: PLACEHOLDER - Observatory external graffiti

entries.title: Iridiscent Gates
entries.body:
Heavy stone gates.

Like most materials without fabrication rights management technology, they cannot be deconstructed with a builder tool.

entries.title: Machine Room
entries.body:
High confidence: this is a machine room.

The structure contains an old generator, perhaps one to two thousand years old. The design is magneto-type, with a commutator to transform the alternating current to direct current. It resembles the designs of French inventor August de Meritens. All construction is of electroformed copper, iron, and bronze; there is no sign of smelting or hot working in any of the components. Insulation is provided by biologically sourced plastics. A battery pile near the generator could be charged by direct current.

The generator has no fuel source and was clearly designed to be turned manually, and it appears the path the operators trod while turning the generator has been cast in concrete—perhaps as a museum exhibit. The sediments below the concrete are rich with food scraps and biological waste.

The machine room also contains equipment for cleaning, warming, cooling, and oxygenating water. Unlike the generator, this equipment has been upgraded to a higher standard, using alternating current and near-premodern materials. Several centuries of inactivity have left this equipment corroded, but reparable.

This generator probably powered water pumps and lighting when this campus was in active use. It was later retired and preserved as an exhibit.

The design may be of use if it becomes necessary to construct and maintain a generator without the benefit of advanced fabrication technology.

entries.title: Mushroom Plug
entries.body: A single plug of polished coral surfaced with concrete, age circa one thousand years. It can be raised or lowered on a column to permit entry into the space below. The mechanism is corroded.

entries.title: Observatory Exterior
entries.body:
Confident estimate: this is an astronomical observatory. It is hollow and must have an entrance.

Structure age: twelve hundred years (inferred from chemical weathering of granite).

Composition: cut granite blocks and concrete. Banding on the granite suggests it was hauled into place by a crane or lift bags. 

Structure surface coating: silicon bronze. The coating was cold-applied by wrapping the structure in a net of wires, then electrifying them and introducing silicon and copper feedstock. The coating electroformed over the stone. 

Induction: the builders had access to electrical power, but not to the hot forging techniques used to smelt large bronze plates. This could be the result of technological development underwater. 

Signs of activity: a gold coating over the bronze has been chipped away over the past several hundred years. Tool marks suggest scavenging or salvage.

Observation: if this is an observatory, the top of the structure should break the surface to allow undistorted imaging of the sky. It does not.

Conclusion: the planetary sea level rose rapidly during the past 1200 years.

entries.title: Orrery
entries.body:
A miniature sky. Also called an orrery. It represents this stellar system, as I represent you.
    
The makers understood that their suns—not their own world, nor even their giant Jovian—lay at the center of things. A fine humility. Perhaps this discovery is why the orrery was preserved as a museum.
    
You might scan the gems within to learn more. The orrery could warn of dangerous cosmic events. And it will be impossible to return home without fixing our position in the galaxy.

entries.title: Telescope
entries.body:
A reflecting telescope, of the type invented by Isaac Newton on Earth.
    
Like every other material encountered in the tower, this telescope was manufactured without the use of high-temperature processes like fire. The reflector is a silver mirror electroplated over quartz.
    
It seems possible that the makers invented telescopes far earlier than humanity did. Perhaps they were inspired by light bending at the boundary between air and water.
    
The telescope was once sealed against water, so that it could produce a clear image. The seals corroded long ago.

entries.title: Unpowered Interface
entries.body:
This is unpowered. It's likely tied to the iridescent looking door blocking access to the inside of the structure. 

Recommendation  - Look around for a power generating station.

entries.title: Viewing Gallery
entries.body:
[PLACEHOLDER] These are bleachers. They were installed at the same time the structure was constructed, but they have been rebuilt several times.

Beings once gathered here to look upon the orrery. 

Did they arrive solemnly, or in a clamor? As solitary pilgrims, or students ushered by their proctors? Did they look to the heavens with awe, or dread? Was there some premonition of their vanishing in the sky?

We certainly cannot say. But whatever caused their disappearance, it happened not long after the year nineteen ninety eight, when the Undertaker threw Mankind off Hell in a Cell, and plummeted sixteen feet through an announcer’s table.

entries.title: Direct  Databank Unlock Entry
entries.body: This is the databank entry unlocked by the "Direct Databank Unlock" test case in the Gameplay Narrative Zoo map. The unlocking story goal is unlocked directly by the trigger volume, for the triggering player only. For other players to see it, they must either unlock it themselves, or a player with it unlocked must upload it after which they can download it from a computer core

entries.title: Multicast Databank Unlock Entry
entries.body: This is the databank entry unlocked by the "Multicast Databank Unlock" test case in the Gameplay Narrative Zoo map. It is unlocked with a World -> Player -> Databank chain. The World story goal triggers the databank to be unlocked on the world, and triggers a player story goal which is propogated to all players that also unlocks the databank. This has the effect of unlocking the databank for all current players, as well as making it available for download for future players.

entries.title: Player  Databank Unlock Entry
entries.body: This is the databank entry unlocked by the "Player Databank Unlock" test case in the Gameplay Narrative Zoo map. The trigger unlocks a player SG for the triggering player, which unlocks the story goal that unlocks this databank entry. For other players to see it, they must either unlock it themselves, or a player with it unlocked must upload it after which they can download it from a computer core. This is functionally the same as triggering the databank unlocking SG directly

entries.title: Scanned Databank Unlock Entry
entries.body: This is the databank entry unlocked by the Scanning the example scannable in the Narrative Zoo. Scanning it unlocks all story goals specified in DA_ZooScanExampleUnlock, one of which (DA_ZooScanDatabankSG) unlocks this entry, one (DA_ZooScanNotificationSG) triggers a notification (DA_ZooScanNotification) and one (DA_ZooWorldScannedRecipeUnlock) unlocks a recipe (DA_ZooScannedRecipe)

entries.title: World Databank Unlock Entry
entries.body: This is the databank entry unlocked by the "World Databank Unlock" test case in the Gameplay Narrative Zoo map. It is unlocked initially for the world and must be downloaded at a computer core to be visible to players

entries.title: Stilt Orb
entries.body: DATABANK ENTRY COMING SOON

entries.title: Marrowbreaches love Nibblers
entries.body:
This is a super cool entry explaining the interaction between Marrowbreach and Nibblersharks. 

How cool!

entries.title: Test Builder Placeable
entries.body: Functional Test Item for Builder Construction

entries.title: Inoculator
entries.body: “Stab” at infection sites on parasitized creatures and force the parasites out. It also can kill the parasites.

entries.title: Test Item
entries.body: [TEST] Test Item

entries.title: The EMT
entries.body: Use the EMT to maintain your Trident and interface with any EMT point you see on the ship. The EMT can turn these interface points, with each of them doing a slightly different contextual function.

entries.title: The Trident
entries.body:
The Trident is your home in the void. It has the following systems:

-Helm: Where you steer and set your speed.
-Sensors: Where you see objects near you, iniitiate long-range scans, and find points of interest.
-Oxygenator: Where you can tune it with the EMT to make sure oxygen flows consistently.
-Power: Where you can swap in battery cells and tune for power efficiency with the EMT.
-Tractor Beam: Where you can pull things into the ship and they automatically get loaded into a tractor beam hold.
-Camera: Where you can swap and steer cameras around the Trident.

Additionally, be sure to check out the other EMT interfaces around the ship where you can do things like drain water from the ship or close doors.

entries.title: The Void experience
entries.body:
Welcome to the Void. Your mission is to deploy a sensor pod inside an alien hive. However, you must find the alien hive, somewhere to the East. Before that, however, you must first repair your Trident, recently lost in a botched mission. To complete the void experience you will need to:

-Fix your Trident by repairing holes, draining it, and powering it on.
-Acquire power from thermal vents to recharge power cells or craft new ones..
-Acquire resources to build the sensor pod, especially Axum Relics which are only found near points of interest.
-Keep your sub from sinking.
-Navigate a dangerous, hard-to-see region.

entries.title: Camer Station
entries.body: The camera station allows you to swap between four cameras positioned on each side of the vessel. You can use them to look around, or press right click to turn on a light, which can be useful for exploring.

entries.title: Control Station
entries.body:
The controls are how you steer your Trident. WASD moves you, Spacebar ascends, and C descends. 

Additionally, you can select speeds by clicking on the arrows on your ship's HUD.

entries.title: Drainage Station
entries.body: Drainage Station

entries.title: Engine Station
entries.body: The engine station allows a player to control their ship's speed from the back of the vessel. It also allows for a player to fully turn off or on their engines with a single button press.

entries.title: Mining Node
entries.body:
The mining site is where you can build a mining platform. Click on the white circle to place a ghost, then provide the necessary materials. 

Once complete, the mining platform will accrue minerals overtime and automatically deposit them in an included storage.

entries.title: Oxygenator
entries.body:
The oxygenator fills your trident with life-giving oxygen. Importantly, the filter can get dirty as the sub gets damaged. 

The damage is represented as a perfect of the filter's life. If it gets too low oxygen will stop flowing.

Be sure to periodically flush the filter using an EMT tool.

entries.title: Power Station
entries.body:
The power station is where you store energy cells, the fuel that keeps your Trident moving. 

If you want to increase your power, you can either add in more power cells (crafted at the fabricator), or you can find thermal vents and build energy recharging stations on them. 

Like many of the Trident's systems, be sure to make sure that the power station is properly maintained. Periodically you must use the EMT to realign the power flow otherwise your power cells will drain faster. Be sure to check it after you take damage.

entries.title: Scanner Station
entries.body:
The scanner station allows you to track large or notable anomalies around the Trident. These show up as icons on the screen, often vaguely indicating what they can be used for or what they might have to offer. 

You can click on a signal once you see it to display its current position and height difference from you, displayed in meters. 

You will also need to periodically need to maintain the sensors with the EMT. To do this you will have to go outside the ship and rotate the two tuning points until the dish is back in alignment. If you fail to do this, the range, speed, and effectiveness of the scanner will depreciate.

entries.title: Sensor Pod Site
entries.body:
The sensor pod site allows you to click the white circle and initiate the building of a sensor pod -- your main mission in the Void. 

Once the ghost of the object appears, add in the necessary materials to build the sensor pod and complete the void experience.

entries.title: Thermal Power Site
entries.body:
Using thermal vents, you can build a recharging station that will restore power to your Trident. 

Click on the white circle, creating the ghost of the thermal vent recharge station. Then provide the necessary materials to complete the project.

Once constructed, simply drive your Trident over the charger to restore power to all inserted power cells.

entries.title: Tractor Beam
entries.body:
The tractor beam is both a tool for pulling things into the ship and also your ship's primary hold. 

To use the tractor beam right click and turn it on, then use the beam to pull in anything within range. Items will automatically be placed into your tractor beam's hold. You can also use it to pull players and creatures towards the ship.

entries.title: Acid
entries.body:
Acid sacs can be removed from acid raions with a survival multitool.

Acid raions prefer shady areas and caves.

entries.title: Copper
entries.body: Bacteria concentrate copper in low-light areas. Search cave roofs, overhangs, shadowed crevices.

entries.title: Gold
entries.body:
Gold precipitates from high-temperature, mineral-rich water around hydrothermal vents. 

Search target: deep, hot caves to the east of the lifepod.

entries.title: Lead
entries.body:
Lead deposits are present northeast of the lifepod, on the seafloor and on rock formations.

High seawater lead levels pose a serious threat to long term human habitation.

entries.title: Lithium
entries.body:
Lithium is bioaccumulated into pearls within the tissue of giant jaws.

Recommend caution while diving the jaw.

entries.title: Oil/Rubber
entries.body:
Lucifer rotsacs can be sacrificed at a fabricator to recover their internal isoprene. 

Rotsacs prefer the bottoms of ravines in warm shallow water, where there is a rich detritus flow to collect.

entries.title: Quartz
entries.body:
Coral domes expel waste silica as they grow. Check the surface and interior of coral domes.

In areas where domes do not grow, quartz may be located in sandy areas.

entries.title: Silver
entries.body:
Silver deposits are associated with silver dolor growth. The silver dolor is a large-gilled organism (resembling a translucent flower) that dwells in shallow caves. Silver ions bind to DNA, making them effective antibiotics on Earth. Spectrogenetic analysis suggests this organism uses silver to stabilize unusual DNA configurations which would normally be nonviable. The adaptive benefit of this chemistry is unknown.

Search target: cave entrances and cave walls to the northwest of the lifepod.

entries.title: Titanium
entries.body:
Loose titanium deposits scattered across the seafloor may be skeletal remnants. Larger nodes are present in cave systems.

Be alert for debris to scavenge and recycle into titanium at a fabricator (or processor).

entries.title: Water Slug
entries.body: Water slugs shelter in anemone grass and on cave floors. Cave floors are likely temporary shelters — slugs may require light for their metabolism.

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Aero Cyst
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Is it air or is it liquid? Is it both? You never knew a cyst could taste this sweet and feel so fluffy and warm. You might never have thought about eating a cyst...and yet, here you are.

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Axum Data Cache
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] A storage device loaded with unintelligble information. Undoubtedly valuable to the culture it came form.

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Axum Relic
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] A relic of the Axum society.

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Ferrofluid Crystal
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Congratulations! You just turned a liquid into a solid. You could make robots with this stuff! Liquid robots? Liquid METAL robots that live underwater? YES!

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Fordite
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Colorful industrial waste. Like the plastic you all got in your brains. Yet, you wave your hands like you just don't care. Great for fuel. Drill baby drill.

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Funky Fruit
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Makes you FEEL music.

entries.title: [PLACEHOLDER] Nutrient Block
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] You don't want to know!

entries.title: Acidic Raion Pouch
entries.body: Gelatinous pouch full of acid with properties useful in fabrication.

entries.title: Aero Cyst
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Is it air or is it liquid? Is it both? You never knew a cyst could taste this soft and fluffy. You might never have thought about eating a cyst...and yet, here you are.

entries.title: Atacamite
entries.body: Atacamite Databank Entry

entries.title: Atacamite
entries.body: Tabular green crystal. Trace substitutions give it anomalous thermo-magnetic behavior, enabling localized cooling.

entries.title: Axum Bacterial Culture
entries.body: Meticulously bred microbe colony used to kickstart Axum technologies such as metal farms. Not reproducible in standard fabricators. Do not eat.

entries.title: Axum Concrete
entries.body: Axum Concrete. Build Axum stuff!

entries.title: Axum Data Cache
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] A crystalline storage device constructued of an incredibly durable material. Inscrutable to our scanners, it still contains important information that the Crypters could use if they aim to rebuild.

entries.title: Axum Relic
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Boxy and  quite heavy, it appears to be an organic material anodized with metal. A series of complex inscryptions cover the surface, with pictographs depicting a series of events on this alien world. Doubtless valuable to the survivors.

entries.title: Celestine
entries.body:
Celestine (strontium sulfate, SrSO4).

A crystalline mineral and useful source of strontium. Celestine dissolves quickly in seawater, but certain microorganisms synthesize it as a shell or ballast.

Local deposits must be the skeletons left by dead colonies of these organisms. The colonies may have overgrown on an artificial strontium source, then died off when the source was removed.

Strontium (Sr), element 38, is a soft and highly reactive metal with applications in radiothermal power generation. Strontium on this planet contains elevated levels of Strontium-90, a highly radioactive, bone-seeking isotope which poses a long-term health risk to human colonists. Strontium-90 is only produced in nuclear fission reactions.

Assessment: do not ingest.

entries.title: Celestine
entries.body: Local source of radioactive Strontium. Burns brilliant red.

entries.title: Charismatic Slime
entries.body: Similar to a syncytial slime mold.

entries.title: Conduit Crystal
entries.body: Kagome optical fibers derived from shock quartz. Naturally occuring wires for light.

entries.title: Conduit Crystal
entries.body: Kagome optical fibers derived from shock quartz. Naturally occuring wires for light.

entries.title: Crab Feces
entries.body: Coprolyte-like deposit of excreta. Extreme energy density; suitable as bioreactor fuel.

entries.title: Creature Enamel
entries.body: Hard ceramic coating. Forms a dense, impermeable surface layer that shields delicate systems from hostile environments.

entries.title: Diamond
entries.body: C. Element 6. Crystalline carbon allotrope with superb physical properties.

entries.title: Enamel
entries.body: Enamel Databank Entry

entries.title: Ferrofluid Crystal
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Congratulations! You just turned a liquid into a solid. You could make robots with this stuff! Liquid robots? Liquid METAL robots that live underwater? YES!

entries.title: Fiber
entries.body: Organic fiber. Closer to plastic or latex than cellulose.

entries.title: Fibrous Pulp
entries.body: Raw, fibrous biomatter. Smells of cinnamon and alcohol.

entries.title: Fordite
entries.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Colorful industrial waste. Like the plastic you all got in your brains. Yet, you wave your hands like you just don't care. Great for fuel. Drill baby drill.

entries.title: Gateway Crystal
entries.body: You can see yourself in this. So many versions of yourself. Of youselves. Of all your selves...there are so many.

entries.title: Gateway Spire
entries.body:
Although technically alive, gateway spires are constructs more akin to termite mounds.

1. Impeller ring
The oldest part of each gateway spire is the impeller ring. This ring contains a loop of conductive, metallic bacterial fibers surrounded by layers of polymer similar to polyaniline. When exposed to electrical current, the bacterial wires create Lorentz force, pushing water through the ring (and pushing the ring in the opposite direction).

2. Tracheal petiole
The stalk (or petiole) supporting the impeller ring resembles a human trachea. Each disc is a colony of soft coral polyps joined by a tough, flexible tissue (the coenenchyme). The hard outer surface is a layer of living diatoms — silica shells covered in tacky mucus. 

3. Constructor species
Gateway spires are assembled by a species of social krill, *Krill krikophore* or the ringbearer krill. Though too small to see unaided, these krill build the entire gateway spire: first laying hoops of bacterial wire, then secreting polymer insulator and gathering polyps of coral and samples of diatom to build the support stalk. The behavior of the ringbearer krill resembles Earth's carpenter wasp.

4. Ecological niche
The gateway spire is a biological tollbooth, drawing in water rich with plankton, krill, and other marine meiofauna. The ringbearer krill feed on these prey and nutrients. Related colonies of ringbearer krill will attempt to align their gateway spires into continuous highways.

5. Power source?
The gateway spire's source of electrical current is not immediately apparent.

Assessment: Possibly useful as a mobility aid.

entries.title: Germanium
entries.body: DATABANK ENTRY COMING SOON

entries.title: Germanium Ingot
entries.body: Ge. Element 32. Solid mass of Germanium for fabricator machining.

entries.title: Gold
entries.body:
Gold (Au), element 79.

Shares many properties with copper and silver.

Highly conductive and ductile. Can form atom-thin wires. Critical for corrosion-resistant electronics, especially in humid environmnents.

Also useful for shielding spacesuits and spacecraft against heat.

Rare—tends to sink to the core of planets during formation. If you cannot access the core of the planet, search for surface deposits formed by bacterial accumulation.

entries.title: Gold
entries.body: Au. Element 79. Useful in corrosion-resistant electronics.

entries.title: Lead
entries.body:
Lead (Pb, element 82).

A dense metal and the heaviest stable element. Found in many ores.

Useful in construction alloys, ballast, radiation shielding, and some very large batteries.

Due to this world’s high metallicity, lead plays an unknown but significant role in the biosphere. All indigenous organisms and water sources are tainted with lead.

Lead can mimic other metals involved in human biology, replacing them in critical reactions. It is a devastating toxin. Long term exposure requires urgent chelation therapy. Take any possible steps to remove lead from your food and water supply.

The presence of organolead compounds in the biosphere of Rakshasa played a major role in the breakdown of the expedition and the renewal of the Obraxis Principle.

entries.title: Lead
entries.body: Pb. Element 82. Heaviest stable element. Slow neurotoxin.

entries.title: Lithium
entries.body:
Lithium (Li), element 3.

The softest and least dense of all metals. Often found dissolved in seawater or in pegmatite deposits, especially near thermal vents.

Useful in lubricants, batteries, advanced glass, nano-welding, quantum physics, and anti-aging drugs. Also a budget air-cleaning element on spacecraft.

Lithium’s role in this world’s biosphere is unknown. In humans it has historically been used as an antidepressant. Despite the advent of cognitive connectome therapy, lithium is still used as a folk remedy on some worlds, despite the risk of permanent side effects.

entries.title: Lithium
entries.body: Li. Element 3. Useful for industry and nuclear physics. Mood stabilizer.

entries.title: Lucifer Rotsac
entries.body:
Tentatively *Rotsac lucifer*, the light-bringing rotsac. A tunicate-like animal which ferments decomposing biomatter inside its body. 

1. Hidden body
The rotsac's larval swimming body transforms into a spherical adult form. Because fermentation does not require oxygen, the rotsac actually stops breathing as it matures.

2. Swollen with hydrocarbons
The rotsac contains isoprene, useful for fabricating rubber and lubricant. High diacetyl levels give the rotsac a strong caramel funk.

3. Bioluminescence
Glow may attract animals (especially sun-seeking microorganisms) to defecate or die on the rotsac. Bioluminescence seems to play an important role in this world's ecology. Regular experiments with light reactions are advised.

4. Symbiote
Often found glued to the cradle shootroot, an unrelated species of starfish-like bottom-dweller that provides the rotsac with an anchor while it grows.

Assessment: useful source of organic polymers for rubber and oil.

Consult your Noetic Advisor to research optimal search areas.

entries.title: Medical Gel Sac
entries.body: Plucked from the stem of an acidic raion. Analysis shows it has medicinal properties.

entries.title: Mineralized Clinker
entries.body: Hardened industrial runoff. Contents vary based on local production during the waning days of the Axum.

entries.title: Necrolei Cyst
entries.body: A massive nodule of acidic compounds. High energy potential.

entries.title: Nickel
entries.body: Ni. Element 28. Hard. Often a sign of meteorite impact. Important nutrient to some organisms.

entries.title: Nickel
entries.body:
Nickel (Ni), element 28.

One of the major exports of supernova explosions. High nickel levels on this world suggest it is young, formed from interstellar clouds seeded by many exploding stars.

Found in nature as an ore with other elements. Nickel sulfide, a major source of nickel, is often found near low-temperature hydrothermal vents. 

Useful for the production of superalloys and nuclear reactor components.

Though nickel is an important part of a balanced diet, this world’s seawater nickel levels pose a health risk. Take precautions to screen excess nickel from your diet and water supply.

Consult your Noetic Advisor to research optimal search areas.

entries.title: Pent
entries.body: Anomaly. No genes detected. Waxy shell full of sulfuric acid.

entries.title: Plasteel
entries.body: DATABANK ENTRY COMING SOON

entries.title: Plasteel Ingot
entries.body: Bonelike matrix of titanium and ceramic. Self-healing. Stronger than strong.

entries.title: Pudding Urchin
entries.body: An unlikely lifeform whose entire body plan resembles and indeed, tastes like, custard. Can be "cooked" in a fabricator to maximize nutrition.

entries.title: Pudding Urchin
entries.body: Pudding Urchin, or Urchin Pudding. It will hurt. But you'll remember the taste.

entries.title: Quartz
entries.body: Silicon dioxide (SiO4). For silica glass and optical memory. Often makes up most of a planet's crust.

entries.title: Raw Ore
entries.body: Rock debris with trace elements.

entries.title: Rubber
entries.body: Flexible, tough insulator derived from biopolymers.

entries.title: Salt
entries.body:
Halite (NaCL), commonly known as salt. A critical nutrient and a favorite flavor in human foodstuffs. 

Nearly four percent of the alien seawater is dissolved salt—far higher than the concentration in human blood. Living things (even on this world) cannot survive this level of salt in their bodies. 

As a result, organisms spend a great deal of energy pumping salt out of their bodies. Some fish use a third of their metabolic energy to pump salt.

A shortage of salt is equally dangerous. Hyponatremia causes hallucinations, seizure, coma and death (easily confused with other syndromes caused by exposure to this alien environment).

Assessment: search for salt on sandy sea bottoms and around dead coral domes. 

Note: pools of salt-heavy brine may occur at the sea floor. Contact with this brine may cause seizures and death. Certain bacteria (halophiles) can thrive in these environments.

entries.title: Salt
entries.body: NaCl. Halite, also known as rock salt. Culinary and sanitation applications.

entries.title: Shell Piece
entries.body:
A chip of calcium carbonate from a living organism’s shell.

Grown by reacting calcium with carbon dioxide on a matrix of bio-vinyl.

The shell was weakened by acid before it finally splintered. This could be a sign of recent climate volatility and acidifying oceans.

Before Earth’s Holocene Collapse and the resulting Expansion, shell-forming organisms helped capture dissolved carbon dioxide from the ocean and return it to the planet’s crust, to one day be released again by volcanoes. 

Modern exoconchology has identified shell-forming organisms on most Earthlike worlds. True thalassic water worlds, with their kilometers-deep oceans and dense layers of pressure ice blocking access to the rock below, do not supply enough calcium for traditional shellforming to evolve.

entries.title: Silver
entries.body: Ag. Element 47. Excellent conductor. Applications in computing, medicine and nanotech.

entries.title: Silver
entries.body:
Silver (Ag), element 47.

The most conductive and reflective of all pure metals. Silver nanoparticles are vital in both medical technology and nanoprinted circuits.

Also important in modern nuclear reactors and high-efficiency solar panels.

Silver accumulation in the body (from exposure in seawater and diet) can cause long term health effects ranging from blue skin to death. This world’s dissolved silver levels are unsafe for human habitation.

Consult your Noetic Advisor to research optimal search areas.

entries.title: Strontium
entries.body: DATABANK ENTRY COMING SOON

entries.title: Sulfur
entries.body: S. Element 16. Useful precursor for strong acids.

entries.title: Titanium
entries.body: Ti. Element 22. Ideal for underwater construction.

entries.title: Troilite
entries.body: Troilite Databank Entry

entries.title: Troilite
entries.body: Iron sulfide mineral. Conductive and thermally reactive, Troilite has applications in a wide range of energy systems.

entries.title: Tungsten
entries.body: Unrefined tungsten.

entries.title: Anita 02
entries.body:
Blackbox: Gottschall, Anita "Anita" 02

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Anita died of drowning caused by sedation. Her lungs contained elevated levels of xenon, which is an anesthetic.

Anita had advanced lead poisoning and severe viremia.

Anita died surrounded by her colleagues. They did not intervene to prevent her drowning.

Anita died about 230,000 hours ago.

Anita’s blackbox transmitted her to an unknown biobed. The reprint process was not supervised by a Noetic Advisor node. 

Anita's status cannot be determined from this blackbox.

entries.title: Axum Door
entries.body: TBD

entries.title: Axum Glyph
entries.body: TBD

entries.title: Climate Lab
entries.body:
The upper observatory tower has been extensively modified.

—Original structure reinforced with corrosion-resistant high-temperature alloys
—Low-voltage, long-duration internal power supply added: probably radiological
—Sensors and digital computers installed

These changes probably occurred more than a thousand years after the observatory's original construction.

Assessment: the observatory was repurposed as an anchor for a long-term monitoring station. The builders expected the station to be abandoned, so left it completely automated.

Recommend entry and data retrieval.

entries.title: Heat Pipe
entries.body:
This pipe once channeled hot water from the titan rockbore below into the turbine above, spinning it to generate electricity. Water flow has been obstructed by bloom growth, preventing the pipe from connecting to the rockbore.

Deposites of corrosive fission products (or "crud") in the pipe indicate that the water flowing through it was once expelled from an active nuclear fission reactor. The water in the rockbore below is now significantly less radioactive, suggesting that the fission source—whatever it might be—has died. The pipe has not been used for at least two centuries.

Assessment: treat bloom growth, then extend pipe to return power plant to operation.

entries.title: Iso
entries.body:
Blackbox:  Isotalo, Rauli "Iso" 01

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Iso died of asphyxiation. He ran out of breathing gas, but sustained consciousness with static apnea for another twelve minutes.

Iso died alone. Iso's AEP suit registered constant touch during and after his death. A bottle of fresh water was forced into Iso's mouth.

Iso died about 236,000 hours ago.

Iso's blackbox transmitted him to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Despite elevated levels of contamination (particularly heavy metals and retroviral DNA), Iso was reprinted without a reset.

Iso's life continued.

entries.title: Iso
entries.body:
Rauli "Iso" Isotalo

Gender: M

Mission role: Survival specialist, celebrity sponsor

Pay rate: No pay.

Biography: Multiple sole survivor of expeditions to Rakshasa and Nyos. Practitioner of Finnish 'BioSisu' discipline. Popular commentator and entertainment personality. Close working relationship with partners Udal Singh and Melaku Sansar. Unable to experience positive emotion in the absence of mortal danger. 

Notes: Despite initial positive sentiment, likely to defect from or mutiny against biobed system. May require versional discipline.

entries.title: Mel
entries.body:
Melaku "Mel" Sansar

Gender: M

Mission role: Survival specialist, celebrity sponsor

Pay rate: No pay.

Biography: Survivor of Rakshasa expedition (after blackbox retrieval by Rauli Isotalo). Born in Kampung Bintang to Ethiopian parents, but changed name to Mongolian patronymic. Childhood friend of Udal Singh. Beloved entertainment personality due to long-suffering relationship with Rauli Isotalo. Emotionally evasive, capable of intense focus bordering on dissociation.

Notes: RASA candidate.

entries.title: Mel
entries.body:
Blackbox: Sansar, Melaku "Mel"

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Mel asphyxiated. His air tanks were torn from his AEP suit by an attacker.

Mel died in the same incident as two coworkers.

Mel died about 236,000 hours ago.

Mel's blackbox transmitted him to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Mel was reprinted without a reset.

Mel's life continued.

entries.title: Metal Farming
entries.body:
Electrowinning, or 'metal farming', is the extraction of dissolved metal ions from seawater. Electric current transmitted through a wire or pillar attracts positively charged metal ions, which form a solid plate on the wire.

Due to the high metallicity of Proteus, the seas contain near limitless quantities of dissolved metal. The Axum apparently developed electrowinning to gather metals long ago, well before fire or high-temperature smelting. They may have been inspired by bacterial fibers which attract metal ions as insulation and armor. If the Axum possess an electrical sense (like many ocean organisms) they would be able to detect electrical current running through these fibers, which would lead them to the development of wires and basic generators.

Operating instructions:
—Ensure the metal farm is powered
—Insert a sample of the desired metal into the unit as a catalyst
—Wait for a deposit to form
—Shatter the deposit and collect the deposited metal

entries.title: Observatory
entries.body:
This tower was likely built as an observatory (best fit theory) or an above-water forge (low probability alternative). The upper tower has been extensively rebuilt.

- Approximately 1000 years old; continuously renovated until circa 200 years ago when the upper tower was modified and the site abandoned
- Built from bioconcrete poured over a metal framework, then electroplated with corrosion-resistant alloys
- Fine grain structure indicates the metal was cold worked, without forging or smelting
- Original design included an electrical system for pumps and gates, predating human commercial electricity by 650 years
- Due to its later construction, the upper tower should be scanned and analyzed separately.

Evidence suggests this tower was built to raise telescopes above water for weather and sky observation. Structures in the interior may have been involved in mapping and modeling the local solar system.

Assesment: locate and repair original power supply to open the observatory. Pioneer-built generators are incompatibile with the alien electrical grid.

entries.title: Orrery
entries.body:
This model of an alien solar system contains two stars, seven planets, and three moons.

—Two binary stars orbiting in physical contact. Proposed names: "Eido" and "Thea". Observations at the sea surface suggest they are class M0V red dwarfs, smaller, colder, and longer-lived than Earth's sun.
—A small, rapidly orbiting world close to the stars
—A planet with an elliptical orbit that swings close to the stars, then climbs far outward
—A gas giant with three large moons, one of which is this world (proposed name: Proteus)
—A tiny planet which shares the gas giant's orbit, racing ahead of it
—A large, rocky world 
—A cold ice giant with an inclined orbit
—An ice giant with a very large ring system

Nearby stars are also indicated by lights around the orrery, including a pulsar. Pulsars can be used as lighthouses to fix a star's position in the galaxy. Consult the Noetic Advisor for further analysis.

Assessment: this orrery is probably a historical site. It was built by Axum with an advanced but pre-modern understanding of astronomy. Until a colony observatory can be constructed, it represents the best available information about the Eido-Thea system and the worlds near Proteus.

entries.title: Power Plant
entries.body:
This enormous electrical power plant is a hybrid methane/hydroelectric design. Analysis suggests it has been refitted and redesigned several times.

—Originally a hydroelectric turbine, powered by hot water from the titan rockbore below
—Redesigned to burn methane (note liquid methane storage tanks and large air intake/exhaust stacks)
—Generates electricity compatible with Axum power grid; at peak output, capable of powering a major city
—All methane-burning components apparently sabotaged circa 250 years ago; plant abandoned

Assessment: the plant can be returned to operation if key components are replaced and the heat pipe is reconnected to the titan rockbore below.

entries.title: Power Plant Turbine
entries.body:
Protein-marker scan complete. Analyzing tensors.

Critical component of the Axum power plant codenamed "Karakorum" by pioneers. Before its sudden destruction, this turbine captured hot water flow to turn the generator.

—Original turbine replaced by more advanced alloy upgrade approximately 300 years ago; coincides with switch to methane-burning power 
—Turbine destroyed by thermite fire 250 years ago: probable sabotage
—Fabricating new turbine blades in place with the builder tool will require huge amounts of manganese alloys
—If repaired, turbine could be linked to the titan rockbore below and power plant restarted

Assessment: find source of necessary material for repairs with habitat builder tool, then connect turbine to hot water source and restart power plant

entries.title: Reaper Statue
entries.body: A statuette of the 4546b reaper leviathan, a predator made famous by Ryley Robinson's memoir 4546B: SURVIVING THE IMPOSSIBLE. As of last survey, flourishing in the ecologically renewed oceans of its homeworld. Sequenced and sampled for possible conservation transplant and use as a keystone species in terraformed oceans.

entries.title: Rosetta Stone
entries.body:
The "Rosetta Stone," named for an important archaeological discovery from pre-Expansion Earth, is a titanium alloy monolith that contains the same message in two alien languages. Presumably its purpose is to teach one species' language to the other.

—Approximately 250 years old
—Structure mimics Architect block construction as discovered on 4546b
—Emplaced on limestone tabletop above water-carved alcoves
—Projected lifespan: unknown

The known text on the monolith is written in Architect Linear, first translated by Architect devices discovered on 4546b. Architect Linear is remarkable for its extremely clear, unambiguous grammar and lack of context: it behaves like a programming language designed to compile on a wide range of hardware. Since first contact on 4546b, all Alterra intelligences have been programmed to translate Architect Linear for their users. It is unlikely that Architect Linear can be spoken aloud.

The unknown text is written in a glyph system provisionally designated Axum Radial A. This system is logographic: each glyph represents an entire word or concept. Discovering additional glyphs will help translate more complex Axum texts.

Conclusion: the Axum left this monolith as a message to Architect visitors. Be alert for signs of Architect activity, including robotic constructs and samples of kharaa bacterium.

entries.title: Ruby
entries.body:
Blackbox: T. "Ruby" Anar, 02

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Ruby died of thoracic trauma. Her AEP suit was penetrated by several uranium-tipped tusks.

Ruby died alone.

Ruby died about 236,000 hours ago.

Ruby's blackbox transmitted her to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Ruby was reprinted without a reset.

Ruby's life continued.

entries.title: Singh
entries.body:

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Singh died of cervical dislocation. He was picked up and moved rapidly. Contralateral pressure was applied to his head and spine.

Singh died alone.

Singh died about 240,000 hours ago.

Singh's blackbox transmitted him to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Despite elevated levels of contamination (particularly heavy metals and retroviral DNA), Singh was reprinted without a reset.

Singh's life continued.

entries.title: Singh
entries.body:
Blackbox: Singh, Udal "Singh" 03

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Singh died of thoracic trauma. He was knocked against metal and lost consciousness. His chest was compressed by a vise or claw until his lungs were too bruised to function.

Singh died near two coworkers.

Singh died about 236,000 hours ago.

Singh's blackbox transmitted him to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Despite elevated levels of contamination (particularly heavy metals and retroviral DNA), Singh was reprinted without a reset.

entries.title: Singh
entries.body:
Udal "Singh" Singh

Gender: M

Mission role: Survival xenobiologist

Pay rate: Bagatur [hero, knight; expired Mongolian Free States trademark for a specialized/valuable worker] 

Biography: Sikh born in the Kampung Bintang. Extreme risk-taker, but unlike partner Rauli Isotalo, not motivated by thrill. Biobed imaging diagnoses him with a possible amygdala lesion, leaving him unable to correctly sense danger. Strongly motivated by curiosity and excitement.

Notes: Good test case for therapeutic reprinting.

entries.title: Tuba
entries.body:
Blackbox: T. "Tuba" Tobukha, 02

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Tuba died of penetrating trauma. She was impaled by a metal spear.

Tuba died near two coworkers.

Tuba died about 236,000 hours ago.

Tuba's blackbox transmitted her to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Despite elevated levels of contamination (particularly heavy metals and retroviral DNA), Tuba was reprinted without a reset.

Tuba's life continued.

entries.title: Tuba
entries.body:
T. "Tuba" Tobukha

Gender: F

Mission role: Cook, dietician's mate

Pay rate: Nökhör [frequently self-described as "underpaid"]

Biography: Colony cook, highly proficient with both fabricator and traditional food preparation. Exposed to dangerous levels of heavy metals and retrovirus due to habit of sampling Protean substances for flavor. History of violence in the context of organized crime. Complains constantly, performs consistently.

Notes: RASA candidate.

entries.title: Tuba
entries.body:
Blackbox: T. "Tuba" Tobukha, 01

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Tuba died of dehydration. She was cornered in a compartment by a marrowbreach outside.

Tuba died alone.

Tuba died about 235,000 hours ago.

Tuba's blackbox transmitted her to an unknown biobed. No return telemetry was received. 

Tuba's status cannot be determined from this blackbox.

entries.title: Upper Control Room
entries.body:
All major power plant systems are connected to this main control room. Once repaired, the plant can be restarted here. The controls must be configured correctly or the plant will abort startup.

Spectral analysis detects alien language outside the human visual range. Recommend adaptation to improve your visual range before attempting to startup.

entries.title: Zip
entries.body:
Blackbox: Malk, Tziporah "Zip" 02

Parsing blackbox data. Outputting natural language report.

Tziporah died of electrical shock. Current traveling through her hand caused ventricular fibrillation and muscle damage, which her AEP suit was unable to correct.

Tziporah died surrounded by colleagues.

Tziporah died about 245,000 hours ago.

Tziporah’s blackbox transmitted her to a biobed in a nearby base camp. Due to a recent reset, Tziporah's body was nearly free of contaminants.

Tziporah's life continued.

entries.title: Are we becoming animals?
entries.body:
GANZO: Huh? Wh...ow.

TWINKLE: Oh, thank God. I thought I'd killed you.

GANZO: You hit me with the Tadpole!

TWINKLE: And then I had to sedate you. You thought Ruby was in that cave. You ccould hear her blackbox. I couldn't hold you back.

GANZO: She needed me to help her. She was sick...

TWINKLE: Ganzo, I heard it too.

GANZO: She was real? And you stopped me — 

TWINKLE: No. It wasn't real. But I heard it. Think about it.

GANZO: Oh...oh no. You too? You've been in the water long enough...

TWINKLE: Yeah. Me too. [big breath] Well, at least I'll get to hear the same voices you do. I don't have to be scared of you any more.

GANZO: Scared of me. Is that why you had a tank of xenon ready to tranquilize me?

TWINKLE: I am scared of you all the time, Ganzo. You are a sick man who could kill me with his bare hands. 

GANZO: Hey. You could kill me too. We're tool users.

TWINKLE: For now. We're turning into...into animals, aren't we? Both of us.

GANZO: Okay. Well...let's talk about it. Let's figure out what to do. Animals can be good to each other. Animals communicate. Even the ugly ones.

TWINKLE: [laughing]

GANZO: I didn't mean you.

TWINKLE: I know. You don't mean me.

entries.title: Do Not Reprint
entries.body:
GANZO: Oh ****! Oh ****. 

TWINKLE: Easy. Easy. You're back.

GANZO: Oh. Twinkle. Oh man. I was...I gave my air to a dead crab.

TWINKLE: You all lost it. I think the hot water triggered your Masefield...

GANZO: Is everyone okay?

TWINKLE: No.

GANZO: No? "No" how bad?

TWINKLE: NoA marked everyone else 'do not reprint'. It's just us now.

GANZO: Oh, man...Ruby? Did she...?

TWINKLE: No. Not here. I'm sorry.

GANZO: I gotta go find her!

TWINKLE: Okay. Yes. I'll come with you. Where do we start?

GANZO: She's...if she resets, she'd print at Sleep Bay 3. But it takes days sometimes...

TWINKLE: I've never been outside before, Ganzo. I'm not adapted. I don't even have fins.

GANZO: We can't...I can't make you wait days. Not if I'm unreliable. We have to find the Jubilee.

TWINKLE: We'll come back for her, okay?

GANZO. Yeah. Yeah. We'll come back.

entries.title: MANTIS: Alien Civilization Discovered
entries.body:
My name is Zygimantis Tarvydas. I am a survivor of the Mongolian Independent States, a colonist aboard the starship Cicada, and a debt slave to the Alterra Corporation. I am with my friends Rauli Isotalo, Udal Singh and Melaku Sansar. Of all the things I have seen and done...this thing is the most important.

I leave this marker as testament that we discovered the remnants of an alien civilization on this world we call Proteus. Let our names be remembered by the workers and the pioneers.

The computers call these aliens 'Axum'. Something has happened to their civilization...the survivors guard these ruins, but they have been reduced to scavengers: Tailings.

I think maybe the virus did it. Seized control of them and drove them mad, as it drives us mad. But I do not know. All I know is that I am here, with my friends, looking on the work of alien minds.

entries.title: MANTIS: Getting through it
entries.body:
MANTIS: We could just blast through.

SINGH: Yeah, and if we're lucky, Nahema kills us and fills our biobeds with concrete before the Tailings get to us. This is their history. We can't blow it up!

ZIP: They do take good care of the exterior...what if the electrical system still works? We could hook up our own generator.

MANTIS: Unless you can find me a wiring diagram we'd fry the whole system. You ever see a metal fire underwater?

ZIP: What if there ARE wiring diagrams? Instructions right in front of us? And we just can't see them?

MANTIS: Did your hot springs tell you about this?

SINGH: Actually...she might be right. The Axum see with a combination of sonar and polarized light. Some of their writing might be invisible to us. We'd need to modify our scanners to look for it. Or our eyes.

ZIP: Ha! 

MANTIS: All right. Let's ask Sophie if we can try out her super scanner. PDA, activate the base camp waypoint. Singh, drop a recording here in case we get reset.

entries.title: Masefield Syndrome
entries.body:
TWINKLE: Hey...

GANZO: Yeah.

TWINKLE: Is the apartment sized squid outside keeping you up too?

GANZO: It's talking to me.

TWINKLE: What?

GANZO: It's saying...come into me and we will dwell in wonder and glory forever. Ow!

TWINKLE: Sorry. I thought you were...the Masefield.

GANZO: I'm joking. I don't hear much when I'm out of the water.

TWINKLE: I keep imagining those tentacles reaching up through the moonpool...

GANZO: ...through the doors and the bulkheads...

TWINKLE: ...grabbing us and taking us...

GANZO: ...to dwell in wonder and glory...

TWINKLE: ...the names of its two very hungry children...

[laughter]

GANZO: Those things killed my wife once.

TWINKLE: It's okay. We're trauma bonding. 

GANZO: Sometimes the call feels like this...this yearning to be close. I can see why the Jubilee think the planet wants to help us. It's strong.

TWINKLE: Look, as an astronomer, let me tell you that nothing good ever comes from attraction. It's unstable.

GANZO: What?

TWINKLE: If planets just went where they were attracted they'd fall into the sun. If stars went where they were pulled they'd fall into the black hole at the middle of the galaxy. It's the resistance that makes the universe work. The balance between being pulled and trying to get away. When something draws you in, you say: "no, wait. Not yet." Whatever Masefield is, it's not something you're just supposed to obey. Or everything in this ocean would just go and die. Like back in the hot cave.

GANZO: See, this is why you're not married.

[loud call]

TWINKLE: What was that?

entries.title: NAHEMA: Why
entries.body:
Sophie. I'm sorry I destroyed your colony. Please listen: I did it because you couldn't. Because you're a good leader and you never give up on the mission.

And I know where this mission will end.

NoA is going to start printing duplicates.

You, and me, and Zip, and Mantis, and Mogami's kids and Thing One and Thing Two and all the rest. Every morning the biobeds will print ten thousand of us,. Every day we'll maximize our KPIs and OKRs and NoA's utility function will say: these people are happy and healthy, mission complete. Humanity is thriving.

Then in the evening we'll all climb into the beds and NoA disintegrates and resets us. A perfect day. Forever. No more mutinies. No more Masefield. No aging, no retirement, no waste. Just peak performance.

But NoA couldn't do that on its own. NoA would need human help to bypass its restrictions.

And you'd help it, Sophie. I know you would. If it was the only choice left — you'd do whatever it takes to keep your crew alive.

So I had to stop you.

entries.title: Powerplant is Dead
entries.body:
The powerplant is dead. Spies set fires in its parts. Relatives say the Viceroys did this. Why? Our claws hurt from work. We cannot fix it. We cannot become a molt which can fix it. Sound says this is happening everywhere. The message cables are cut. Why? Xanadu has gone to war against itself.

It is hot without the pumps.

entries.title: SINGH: Axum 1
entries.body:
Field notes, Udal Singh, observing the Axum Tailings in the Karakorum ruins. My reprint site is base camp near the observatory.

This time they killed me right away. They really don't like us coming into the ruins. They are obviously capable of communication and social behavior. Why are they so damn hostile?

According to Nahema's research we are the only life on this world not infected by the Proteavirus. Maybe the virus provokes violence against the uninfected? I don't know. It feels too convenient. "The natives hate us because they're sick!" More likely they have good reason to distrust humans. 

And they know we come back when they kill us. So maybe they are communicating. Maybe they're trying to teach us: you cross this line, you die.

entries.title: SINGH: Axum 2
entries.body:
Field notes, Udal Singh.

The Karakorum ruins imply a civilization that developed to the same level as pre-Expansion Earth. They had electrical power, industrial metals, digital computers, orbital spaceflight, the whole suite. But they arrived to the same place along a different path.

They got electricity FIRST. I think they — hang on a minute, I've got to hide — I think the Axum themselves can sense electricity. Like sharks. And they figured out, hey, these long bacterial fibers — these things carry an electrical current. Then some clever Axum wrapped those fibers up into a generator. And once you've got a generator you can make your own electricity and start pulling metal from the seawater. Farming the sea to make tools. All without one spark of fire.

Imagine it. Undersea cities as old as Troy or Harappa, but with telegraph lines and electric lighting. Thousands of years before the first flame. To us, electricity means modernity. To the Axum...it's as old as bronze.

entries.title: SINGH: Axum 3
entries.body:
Field Notes, Udal Singh. Think I'm about to die again.
 
Listening to that thing pick through the wreckage of my Tadpole. I think it's having fun.

Humanity exterminated most land-based megafauna before we scratched our first letters into clay. But the Axum never cleared their oceans. They made it all the way to digital computers while coexisting with predators that could eat them like cocktail shrimp.

Why? Is it something about the ocean? Just too big and deep? 

Or did the Axum have a different relationship with nature? I can already hear you sneering, Iso. "They're lobster aliens, not elves. Don't turn them into a parable."

Imagine you evolved in an ocean full of leviathans like Grabby out there. Hundreds of generations with those tentacles strangling your nightmares. Then one day you invent the explosive-tipped harpoon and you can fight back. Wouldn't you go out and kill them all? Like we killed the sabertooth tiger and the...I don't know, the automobile?

So why didn't the Axum do it? Why is Grabby still here to pull my Tadpole to pieces?

entries.title: SINGH: How to see the unseen
entries.body:
ISO: Okay...the screen's on but it's just noise.

SINGH: It's noise to us. To Axum, it's a polarized light display.

ISO: How do I read polarized light? 

SINGH: Well, either we fabricate some kind of goggles, or...

ISO: Or we find one of Anita's angel combs. And change our eyes.

SINGH: Do you think she's on to something?

ISO: I don't think. I just do things.

SINGH: PDA, print this to quartz.

ISO: What are *you* thinking?

SINGH: Leaving myself clues. In case—you know.

entries.title: SOPHIE: Depth module lost
entries.body:
MANTIS: Sorry, boss, what's going on?

SOPHIE: Wait one. Ruby — it's Butcher. Are you with your Tadpole?

RUBY [on intercom]: *Right outside, boss. What's up?*

SOPHIE: Go direct southeast from the research base, about 500 meters, until you find the Cicada wreckage on the tall mesa. Flash your lights and make some noise. Ram it if you have to.

RUBY [on intercom]: *Copy. Steer southeast, 500 meters, same depth. Can I ask why?*

SOPHIE: Tuba, Singh and Mel just printed back here. They were testing the upgraded scanner and resonator when Axum Tailings attacked them. Killed them and took Iso. He's still alive. I've tracked his blackbox to the Cicada wreck nearby.

RUBY: [on intercom] *Uh...copy. Can't we just wait for them to finish him off?*

SOPHIE: He's got Nahema's super scanner. She'll murder me if I lose it. Try to scare the Tailings off. I'll get a team out there to recover his effects.

RUBY [on intercom, crackling]: *Boss, I've picked up a couple tails. Looks like — two adult needlers — need to evade — *

MANTIS: Boss, her Tadpole has our only depth module.

SOPHIE: Oh, Christ. [muttering] Come on, Ruby...PDA, log my conduct here for kurultai review.

entries.title: SOPHIE: In pursuit of Nahema
entries.body:
To any survivors of the Coral Gardens Habitat — 

I am pursuing Nahema towards the World Tree. She wants to eradicate humanity on Proteus, and she'll destroy the regional ecosystem to do it. I believe she's going to poison the Tree.

Do not believe anything she tells you. She is determined to kill us all.

I am leaving supplies for anyone who comes after me. If you lose my trail, assume that I failed. Find Nahema. Kill the tree.

Good luck. Butcher out.

entries.title: TUBA: New recipe
entries.body:
TUBA: Do you think they were like us?

SINGH: No.

TUBA: But look at this. It's a house. They lived in houses, they went to jobs, they ate food. How different could they be?

SINGH: Nahema thinks they evolved with the Masefield syndrome. The way we evolved with...day and night, or family.

TUBA: So what? They were all crazy about the tree?

SINGH: Well, Sophie thinks Masefield killed them all. And I don't know who's right.

TUBA: Maybe their cooks went on strike and they starved to death. Hey, do you think they tasted good?

SINGH: Tuba!

TUBA: What? Don't you like shrimp? PDA, open recipe book.

SINGH: TUBA!

entries.title: Turbine Repair Plan
entries.body:
NAHEMA: Why do you need *me* to fix this?

MANTIS: So. The scanner doesn't work. The crystal structure of the turbine blades depends on the growth of bacterial fiber. The scanners see it but don't understand it. No scan, no blueprint, no repairs.

NAHEMA: Oh.

MANTIS: Oh?

NAHEMA: This is actually an interesting problem. Let me write this down.

MANTIS: Isn't it? I imagine they cold-forged these blades from some kind of metal clabber...

NAHEMA: ...yeah. Okay. We're going to have to modify the scanner. Do you have a mod station at base camp?

MANTIS: I told Zip to set it up, but she's still...

NAHEMA: Yeah. We should talk about that.

MANTIS: Someone destroyed this place on purpose. We should talk about that too.

entries.title: TWINKLE: Tailings
entries.body:
TWINKLE: [breathing hard] Holy hell.

GANZO: [breathing hard] Yeah. Damn. Damn, we made it!

TWINKLE: When those things — 

GANZO: Tailings.

TWINKLE: When the Tailings came after us all I could think was: we aren't going to reprint. This is it. I finally get to go outside and the first thing I do is die on a spear.

GANZO: Let's sort this cache into 'take' and 'bury'. Why didn't Sophie let you outside?

TWINKLE: Why didn't I ever do any work, you mean?

GANZO: Hey. You always seemed cool. I just never saw you...do anything.

TWINKLE: I'm keeping something secret. If I ever got sick and reset, Sophie wasn't sure she could convince me to keep it secret again. So...I had to stay safe.

GANZO: Ruby gets killed all the time. Really freaks me out.

TWINKLE: Yeah...she asked me to listen to a recording once.

GANZO: Thanks for tricking me into leaving her.

TWINKLE: What?

GANZO: I mean it. If we're all marked 'do not reprint', so is she. She didn't reprint. I would've gone looking for her and...

TWINKLE: The Masefield would've got you.

GANZO: I can hear the tree, Twinkle. It wants me to go into the sea and...feed myself to it.

TWINKLE: If you do that you'll never see Ruby again.

GANZO: Yeah. I know. Anyway, we can't stay here. Tailings don't like the open plains. We can build there and start scanning for the Jubilee.

entries.title: What were they eating?
entries.body:
TWINKLE: Ganzo, I don't think this is the Jubilee.

GANZO: They're us.

TWINKLE: That's me. I guess NoA wants an astronomer in the mix.

GANZO: You figure there's a sleep bay nearby? 

TWINKLE: Yeah. Same as our group. It printed out some colonists and told them to make a go at it. But these poor people...

GANZO: They don't have any adaptations. Look at you, your legs are like sticks. You're starving.

TWINKLE: They can't digest the food. No angel comb to donate the enzymes. I don't see any growbeds...what are they...oh my God. Ganzo, let's go.

GANZO: Wait. Wait. We could ask for help. Maybe they've got communications, maybe they've heard from the ship.

TWINKLE: Look at the biobeds, Ganzo! Look at that!

GANZO: Yeah. I see it. It won't work long term, though. The biobeds need feedstock like anything else...why waste it making food? It'd make more sense to reprint yourself every day...unless the point was to test how long you could live on a diet of biobed meat...oh, man.

TWINKLE: You are remarkably calm about this.

GANZO: A lot of animals eat their young under stress.

entries.title: ZIP: Met my old friend
entries.body:
NAHEMA: What was in those giant tanks?

ZIP: Huh? Oh. Um—probably methane. Natural gas.

NAHEMA: I thought this was a hydroelectric plant.

ZIP: Originally, yeah, but the old Axum added a methane burner about...um...hang on, let me check my databank.

NAHEMA: It's okay. Hey - really. It's okay. Where's Mantis?

ZIP: He's up there, on the broken turbine. He thinks you can help him fix it.

NAHEMA: Up that big arch? All right.

ZIP: So...before I got reset...we were friends?

NAHEMA: Yeah. We were.

ZIP: Is it okay if I write that down?
